exemplar.title:
What is the smallest volume piece of wood from which a full-scaled set of 16 pawns from the
World Chess Championship chess set can be carved, optimised through calculus and geometrical
modelling?

detailedExemplarData.title:
What is the smallest volume piece of wood from which a full-scaled set of 16 pawns from the
World Chess Championship chess set can be carved, optimised through calculus and geometrical
modelling?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

versions.error: Durable Object reset because its code was updated.

strengths: The exploration follows a clear logical sequence with an identifiable introduction, body and conclusion.
Use of headings, diagrams and tables close to their discussion enhances reader navigation.
weaknesses: Lengthy historical digressions interrupt the investigative flow.
Redundant statements of the aim and duplicated material reduce conciseness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work is coherent and logically developed, with an organized structure and mostly relevant content. However, interruptions from historical asides, repeated aims and occasional backtracking prevent it from reaching the highest band for exceptional coherence and conciseness. A score of 3 reflects its well-organized but not yet exceptionally concise presentation.

strengths: Notation (e.g. Ï, integrals, subscripts) is used correctly and consistently in most places.
Multiple representations (tables, graphs, algebraic expressions and 3D screenshots) support and clarify the argument effectively.
weaknesses: Graphs and figures sometimes lack scales or labels, reducing interpretive clarity.
Notational inconsistencies (mis-typed limits, conflicting decimal values) sporadically hinder precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and notation are mostly consistent and clear, and a variety of representations are used effectively to communicate the modelling process. Minor slips in scaling, labeling and symbol consistency prevent a higher band. A score of 3 accurately reflects good logical structure and mostly consistent notation without the flawless clarity needed for band 4.

strengths: The choice of a personal chess context and bespoke packing strategy shows significant independent thinking.
Trial-and-error placement of interpolation points and the development of multiple layouts demonstrate good creativity and initiative.
weaknesses: The mathematical techniques themselves are standard IB methods without novel extensions.
No entirely new mathematical tool or approach is developed beyond familiar course territory.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration clearly reflects the studentâs personal interest and initiative, with significant evidence of independent thought. However, the approach remains within the realm of conventional IB techniques and lacks the exceptional ingenuity required for the highest band. Thus, a score of 2 is appropriate.

strengths: Reflections on the initial hypothesis and efficiency comparisons demonstrate meaningful reflection.
Thoughtful commentary on packing improvements shows an understanding of results.
weaknesses: Underlying assumptions, error sources and alternative modelling choices are not probed in depth.
No discussion of real-world or future implications limits the scope of the evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides meaningful reflections and basic critical analysis of results and processes. However, reflection does not reach the degree of substantial, insightful critique or comprehensive evaluation of implications. A band-2 score reflects this thoughtful yet not fully developed reflection.

strengths: A broad range of relevant techniques (volumes of revolution, polynomial interpolation, matrix methods, 3D modelling and packing efficiency) are applied to address the aim.
Derivations and numerical results cohere into a convincing final efficiency-packing ratio, showing effective mathematical modelling.
weaknesses: Minor computational shortcuts and rounding inconsistencies occur in several integrals and matrix calculations.
Some integration bounds and notational groupings are inconsistent, requiring more rigorous checking.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration employs a comprehensive suite of IB-level mathematical methods effectively and demonstrates thorough understanding. While minor inaccuracies and occasional shortcuts are present, the depth and breadth of application across calculus, interpolation, spatial reasoning and optimisation warrant an elevated mark. Assigning 5 reflects the studentâs strong, mostly correct use of mathematics, slightly above the effective-level band but acknowledging that flawless precision (band 6) was not fully achieved.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations generally precede calculations, and justification is given for choices such as piecewise interpolation and extra points.  Step-by-step layouts for sample integrations are logical.  Nevertheless, sporadic typographical errors (e.g. mis-typed limits) reduce clarity at a few points, so the strand fits âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ.

Derivations are mostly correct; the piecewise function integrates to a plausible single-pawn volume, and efficiency calculations are coherent.  Minor inaccuracies (rounding, copy errors in limits) are present but do not undermine overall validity.  Hence the student demonstrates good understanding with predominantly correct work, warranting band 4 rather than the flawless band 5/6.

The student employs tables of coordinates, Desmos graphs, algebraic expressions, 3-D Tinkercad screenshots and numeric summaries (EPR values).  These representations support one another and help clarify the argument; however, scales are sometimes missing and images occasionally crowd the page, so the use is âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ.

The exploration follows a clear sequence â introduction of aim, construction of mathematical model, optimisation and packing comparison, and conclusion â so the reader can follow the investigation without major gaps. Transitions between measuring, modelling, integrating and evaluating layouts are explicit, showing a logical thread. However, some sections drift into lengthy historical digressions (e.g.
Rungeâs phenomenon and Vandermonde) that interrupt the flow, and there are occasional jumps where computational results are presented with little verbal linkage. These weaknesses prevent the work from reaching the very highest standard of âexcellentâ coherence.

Introducing efficiency-packing ratio, trial-and-error placement of additional interpolation points, and developing multiple packing layouts show creativity and initiative above a routine treatment.  However, no entirely new mathematical tool is created and the modelling remains within familiar HL territory, matching âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ creativity.

Results are interpreted through efficiency ratios and qualitative comments on wasted space, showing thoughtful analysis.  Still, sensitivity to measurement error, numerical integration accuracy or limitations of polynomial modelling is only briefly mentioned, so the analysis is thoughtful but not âdeep and insightfulâ.

The conclusion identifies the preferred block size and recognises that the computed minimum is theoretical.  Implications for real manufacturing or possible refinement are not explored, and no future investigation is proposed, so the evaluation is clear but not comprehensive.

The student revisits the initial hypothesis regarding wasted wood and comments on how results contradict expectations.  Reflections are embedded at key junctures (after EPR comparisons) but seldom probe underlying assumptions or alternative modelling choices in depth, giving âmeaningfulâ but not âsubstantialâ reflection.

Standard mathematical symbols (Ï, â«, subscripts), function notation and units are used correctly in most places and are generally consistent.  Minor slips â such as writing âV = w Ã l Ã hâ twice with slight changes or mis-labelled subscripts â occur but do not seriously hinder meaning, so the descriptor âmostly consistentâ applies.

The investigation is clearly driven by the studentâs enthusiasm for chess; measurements taken from a personal set and experimental physical arrangements demonstrate a genuine personal route through the task, but the approach is not so innovative or distinctive as to merit the top band.

While most content is relevant to determining a minimum block of wood, several extended explanations (full matrix method, repeated EPR definitions) add little to the final answer.  Screenshots and photographs are sometimes duplicated, and lengthy quotations of generic formulae inflate the length.  Therefore the work is âmostly relevantâ but not yet âconciseâ.

Volume of revolution, polynomial interpolation, matrix methods, geometry of cuboids/cylinders and packing efficiency are all solidly relevant to the stated aim and are carried through to a conclusion.  The mathematics is used effectively, but occasional computational shortcuts (single GDC integral shown, limited error checking) limit it to âeffectiveâ rather than âcomprehensiveâ.

Selecting a personally meaningful chess context and devising a bespoke packing strategy shows noticeable independent thought beyond textbook problems.  Still, much of the mathematical technique (volume of revolution, polynomial fitting) follows standard methods without significant original adaptation, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

The student provides a recognisable introduction, body and conclusion, uses headings consistently, and keeps related calculations together. Diagrams, tables and formulae appear close to the discussion that refers to them. Nonetheless, sub-sections sometimes appear in an order that forces the reader to turn pages back (e.g. extra points added after later graphs), and the overall document is longer than necessary, suggesting room for tighter structuring.

Calculus of volumes of revolution and polynomial interpolation are HL-appropriate.  Matrix solution of simultaneous equations and 3-D spatial reasoning also meet HL expectations.  The work is on-level and applied correctly, yet it does not push to specialist university-level material, fitting the descriptor âfully commensurate with course levelâ.

annotations.comment: The repetition of the aim on this page and the following one interrupts the logical flow. Consider consolidating both statements into a succinct aim. Redundancy here reduces conciseness.
The introduction links mathematical investigation to personal passion for chess, demonstrating genuine engagement. This personal context enriches the exploration.
Figure 1 lacks a scale or annotations indicating dimensions. Adding axis labels or measurement markers would clarify the visual model.
The final sentence in the Method section ends abruptly, suggesting text is missing. Ensure each methodological step is fully described.
Re-stating the aim in full on this page duplicates content from page 1. Consolidate to avoid unnecessary repetition and improve conciseness.
The aim paragraph could define specific measurable variables (e.g. height, diameter) more clearly to guide the subsequent modelling steps.
The definition of a piecewise function is verbose. Consider summarizing this concisely and moving formal definitions to an appendix if needed.
In the volumeâofârevolution formula, the start and end coordinates are labeled inconsistently (Î± and h), and the variable h overlaps with height. Use distinct symbols and define them precisely.
The historical digression on Vandermondeâs method, though interesting, interrupts the investigation. Consider moving detailed history to a footnote.
Equation 3 is presented clearly and uses standard polynomial notation appropriately.
The general reduced rowâechelon form table (Equation 4) is included without connecting it explicitly to the pawn interpolation. Clarify how this relates to the specific degree chosen.
The coordinate table repeats the point (0.395, 1.47) twice without justification. Ensure all listed points contribute uniquely to the interpolation.
Graph 1 lacks axis labels and clear scaling. Including units on both axes would improve interpretation of the piecewise modelling.
The explanation of Rungeâs phenomenon is well placed to justify piecewise interpolation and shows thoughtful mathematical reasoning.
The piecewise splicing rationale does not verify continuity at each junction. Check derivative continuity or justify why only function continuity suffices for volume calculation.
Notation in the description â(0, 1, 47)â is misleadingâdecimal points are missing. Consistent use of decimals avoids confusion.
The stepâbyâstep explanation of creating the first polynomial section is clear and logically presented.
The example polynomial solution is shown without detailing how the GDC was used. Briefly describe the GDC steps or settings to enhance reproducibility.
The RREF matrix values are rounded inconsistently (e.g. 3.75992 vs 0.348505). Use uniform significant figures to maintain accuracy.
Reflecting on your trial-and-error placement of points highlights independent thinking and initiative in refining the model.
The piecewise function (Equation 8) uses 1.475 in the first interval, conflicting with earlier 1.47. Ensure segment values match consistently.
The lower limit in the first integral is given as 0.359 instead of 0.395, creating a mathematical inconsistency. Correct the integration bounds.
The phrase âThis volume of wood is impossible but is a mark for how efficient the packing isâ is ambiguous. Rephrase to clarify the theoretical goal versus practical limitations.
The stepâbyâstep setup of the integral (Equation 9) is clear and logically ordered, aiding comprehension of the volume calculation.
The GDC output for \(V_{min}\) is stated without showing key steps. Include a brief outline of the GDC procedure or intermediate values.
The cuboid packing description switches between diameter (2.95 cm) and radius (1.475 cm) without comment. Use consistent measurements or explain conversions.
The 3D cuboid model (Figure 2) is well-rendered and supports the packing argument visually.
The cylinder volume calculation uses an inline âcm<sup>3</sup>â notation instead of consistent LaTeX units. Standardize to \(\text{cm}^3\).
The efficiency ratio for the cylindrical model is not compared to previous ratios in text. Adding a brief comparison would deepen critical reflection.
The Desmos 3D model of stacked cylinders lacks an explicit volume calculation. Include formulas to justify the visual arrangement.
The discussion of wasted space in cylindrical packing is perceptive but could explore implications for real manufacturing constraints.
Figure 6 is placed before its introductory text is complete, forcing the reader to backtrack. Reposition or split text to improve coherence.
The pattern dimension formula uses summed radii but lacks a supporting annotated diagram. Including a schematic would clarify these sums.
Equation 17 lacks parentheses around the denominator, making the EPR expression ambiguous. Use clear grouping notation.
The critical observation that this pattern yields minimal improvement shows thoughtful reflection on packing efficiency.
The description of the âhook ridgeâ pattern is unclear without definitions or diagrams of these features. Provide explicit definitions.
Figure 8âs photograph is low resolution and unscaled. Include measurement references or higher-quality images for clarity.
The final EPR of 52.47% demonstrates effective use of mathematical modelling and packing analysis.
The conclusion summarises results well but omits consideration of human or manufacturing limitations. Discuss potential real-world constraints.
The concluding paragraph effectively reflects on the initial hypothesis and demonstrates critical evaluation of outcomes.
The Works Cited list shows inconsistent formatting between entries. Adopt a consistent citation style throughout.
The final citation is truncated across lines and lacks a complete URL. Verify each reference for completeness and correctness.
